description:
src/lib.rs:236-247 maps HTTP 401 to SecretError::Unavailable with hint 'GCP_ACCESS_TOKEN may have expired; reconstruct the backend with a fresh token'.

But secretx-core/src/lib.rs:976-983 documents Backend as the variant for 'authentication failure, permission denied' and Unavailable as 'retrying may succeed' with 'retry with back-off'. Plain back-off retry on 401 will not succeed without operator action (new token); the type-level signal 'Unavailable -> retry with backoff' misleads programmatic callers.

The deliberate justification (token-expiry recovery via reconstruct) is operationally valid but conflicts with the documented contract. Two paths to resolve:
  (a) Map 401 -> Backend (strict contract conformance). Operators read the error message; programmatic callers correctly abort.
  (b) Update secretx-core's Unavailable doc to explicitly cover 'caller may recover by reconstructing with new credentials' — making this case a sanctioned use of Unavailable.

Until resolved, callers that retry-on-Unavailable in a tight loop will burn through GCP rate limits with the same expired token.

Cross-crate aspect: the contract lives in secretx-core; the deviation lives here. Either change requires touching one or the other.

Trigger: any 401 from GCP SM (expired token, never-valid token, IAM revocation).
Test coverage: tests/map_http_status_401_is_unavailable enforces current behavior — would need updating if (a) is chosen.
title: 401 -> Unavailable conflicts with secretx-core contract semantics
